questions.question:
Read the following paragraph and extract these fields into a JSON object: shares_repurchased_current, shares_repurchased_prior, repurchase_amount_current, repurchase_amount_prior, repurchase_program_remaining. Use null for any field not mentioned in the paragraph.

Paragraph: "For the six months ended December 31, 2025 and 2024, we repurchased 20 million shares and 15 million shares of our common stock for $9.9 billion and $6.3 billion, respectively, through our share repurchase program. All repurchases were made using cash resources. As of December 31, 2025, $47.4 billion remained of our $60 billion share repurchase program. questions.question: NVIDIA reported total revenue of $46.7 billion for Q2 FY2026 (three months ended July 27, 2025), up 56% year-over-year. For Q3 FY2026 (three months ended October 26, 2025), NVIDIA reported total revenue of $57.0 billion, up 62% year-over-year. Is NVIDIA's year-over-year revenue growth rate accelerating or decelerating between Q2 and Q3 FY2026?
questions.expected_answer: Accelerating. The year-over-year revenue growth rate increased from 56% in Q2 FY2026 to 62% in Q3 FY2026, a gain of 6 percentage points, indicating the pace of year-over-year growth is speeding up even from an already high base.

source.section_hint: Revenue discussion, Q3 FY2026 vs Q2 FY2026 year-over-year growth comparison
source.original_text: Revenue was $57.0 billion, up 62% from a year ago and up 22% sequentially.

questions.notes: Calculation: Q2 YoY growth 56% → Q3 YoY growth 62% = acceleration of 6pp. Both figures from respective 10-Q filings.

questions.question: Meta reported total revenue of $47.52 billion for Q2 2025, an increase of 22% year-over-year. For Q3 2025, Meta reported total revenue of $51.24 billion, an increase of 26% year-over-year. Is Meta's year-over-year revenue growth accelerating or decelerating?
questions.expected_answer: Accelerating. The year-over-year revenue growth rate rose from 22% in Q2 2025 to 26% in Q3 2025, an increase of 4 percentage points, indicating growth is picking up pace.

source.section_hint: Q3 2025 financial results overview vs Q2 2025 financial results overview
source.original_text: Total revenue for the third quarter of 2025 was $51.24 billion, an increase of 26% compared to the third quarter of 2024.

questions.notes: Calculation: 22% (Q2) → 26% (Q3) = 4pp acceleration. Both figures stated in their respective 10-Q filings.

questions.question: Microsoft reported that Azure and other cloud services revenue increased 40% in Q1 FY2026 (three months ended September 30, 2025). In Q2 FY2026 (three months ended December 31, 2025), Azure and other cloud services revenue increased 39%. Is Azure's growth rate accelerating or decelerating?
questions.expected_answer: Decelerating. Azure growth slowed from 40% in Q1 FY2026 to 39% in Q2 FY2026, a decline of 1 percentage point, indicating a slight deceleration in growth pace.

source.section_hint: Azure and other cloud services revenue growth, Q1 vs Q2 FY2026
source.original_text: Azure and other cloud services revenue increased 39%.

questions.notes: Calculation: 40% (Q1 FY26) → 39% (Q2 FY26) = 1pp deceleration. Q1 from MSFT_10-Q_2025-10-29, Q2 from MSFT_10-Q_2026-01-28.

questions.question: NVIDIA's Data Center compute revenue was $33.8 billion in Q2 FY2026, up 50% year-over-year. In Q3 FY2026, Data Center compute revenue was $43.0 billion, up 56% year-over-year. Meanwhile, Data Center networking revenue was $7.3 billion in Q2, up 98% year-over-year, and $8.2 billion in Q3, up 162% year-over-year. Which Data Center sub-segment saw greater growth acceleration from Q2 to Q3?
questions.expected_answer: Networking saw greater acceleration. Networking's YoY growth increased by 64 percentage points (98% to 162%), while compute's YoY growth increased by only 6 percentage points (50% to 56%). Networking's acceleration was roughly 10 times larger.

source.section_hint: Data Center compute and networking revenue, Q2 vs Q3 FY2026
source.original_text: Data Center compute revenue was $43.0 billion, up 56% from a year ago and up 27% sequentially. Networking revenue was $8.2 billion, up 162% from a year ago.

questions.notes: Calculation: Compute accel = 56%-50% = 6pp. Networking accel = 162%-98% = 64pp. Networking acceleration far greater. All per-period figures from respective 10-Q filings.

questions.question: Microsoft reported that its Cloud gross margin percentage decreased to 68% in Q1 FY2026 (three months ended September 30, 2025) and further decreased to 67% in Q2 FY2026 (three months ended December 31, 2025). Both declines were attributed to scaling AI infrastructure and growing AI product usage. Is this margin trend improving or deteriorating, and what does it suggest about the near-term cost dynamics of Microsoft's AI investments?
questions.expected_answer: Deteriorating. Cloud gross margin fell from 68% to 67% over two consecutive quarters, indicating that AI infrastructure costs are growing faster than efficiency gains. This suggests Microsoft's heavy AI investment is compressing margins in the near term.

source.section_hint: Microsoft Cloud gross margin percentage, Q1 vs Q2 FY2026
source.original_text: Microsoft Cloud gross margin percentage decreased to 67% driven by continued investments in AI infrastructure and growing AI product usage, offset in part by efficiency gains in Azure and Microsoft 365 Commercial cloud.

questions.notes: Trend: 68% (Q1) → 67% (Q2) = 1pp decline over two quarters. Both filings attribute decline to AI infrastructure costs.

questions.question: Tesla reported that automotive sales revenue increased $1.53 billion, or 8%, in Q3 2025 compared to Q3 2024. However, for the nine months ended September 30, 2025, automotive sales revenue decreased $4.75 billion, or 9%, compared to the same period in 2024. What does this divergence between the quarterly and year-to-date figures tell us about the trend in Tesla's automotive revenue?
questions.expected_answer: Tesla's automotive revenue is recovering. The nine-month decline of 9% reflects weakness in the first half of the year, but the Q3 quarterly increase of 8% indicates a reversal in the second half. The improving quarterly trajectory suggests the earlier downturn may be ending.

source.section_hint: Automotive sales revenue, Q3 2025 and nine months ended September 30, 2025
source.original_text: Automotive sales revenue increased $1.53 billion, or 8%, in the three months ended September 30, 2025 as compared to the three months ended September 30, 2024.

questions.notes: Key insight: Q3 alone is +8% YoY while YTD is -9%, meaning H1 was deeply negative. Q3's positive turn indicates recovery.

questions.question: Meta's capital expenditures including principal payments on finance leases were $17.01 billion in Q2 2025 and $19.37 billion in Q3 2025. Is Meta's capital spending trend increasing or decreasing?
questions.expected_answer: Increasing. Capital expenditures grew from $17.01 billion in Q2 2025 to $19.37 billion in Q3 2025, an increase of $2.36 billion, reflecting continued ramp-up of infrastructure investment.

source.section_hint: Capital expenditures, Q2 2025 vs Q3 2025

questions.notes: Calculation: $19.37B - $17.01B = $2.36B increase quarter-over-quarter. Q2 from META_10-Q_2025-07-31, Q3 from META_10-Q_2025-10-30.

questions.question: JPMorgan Chase reported that in Q3 2025, net charge-offs were $2.6 billion, up $506 million year-over-year, predominantly driven by Wholesale and Card Services. The net addition to the allowance for credit losses was $810 million. The allowance for credit losses stood at $29.1 billion as of September 30, 2025, with a coverage ratio of 1.88% compared to 1.86% in the prior year. Is JPM's credit quality improving or deteriorating?
questions.expected_answer: Deteriorating. Net charge-offs increased $506 million year-over-year, and the firm added $810 million to its loss allowance. The coverage ratio also edged up from 1.86% to 1.88%, indicating JPM expects higher future losses and is building reserves accordingly.

source.section_hint: Credit quality discussion, provision for credit losses, Q3 2025
source.original_text: Net charge-offs of $2.6 billion were up $506 million, predominantly driven by Wholesale and Card Services. The net addition to the allowance for credit losses was $810 million.

questions.notes: Three indicators point to deterioration: rising charge-offs, building allowance, and increasing coverage ratio. All figures from JPM_10-Q_2025-11-04.

questions.question: Alphabet reported Q3 2025 revenues of $102.3 billion, with Google Services revenues increasing $10.5 billion, or 14%, and Google Cloud revenues increasing $3.8 billion, or 34%. Which segment is growing faster on a percentage basis, and which contributed more in absolute dollar terms?
questions.expected_answer: Google Cloud is growing faster on a percentage basis at 34% versus 14% for Google Services. However, Google Services contributed more in absolute dollars at $10.5 billion versus $3.8 billion for Cloud, reflecting Services' much larger revenue base.

source.section_hint: Revenue overview, three months ended September 30, 2025
source.original_text: Revenues were $102.3 billion, an increase of 16% year over year, primarily driven by an increase in Google Services revenues of $10.5 billion, or 14%, and an increase in Google Cloud revenues of $3.8 billion, or 34%.

questions.notes: Cloud percentage growth (34%) > Services (14%), but Services absolute increase ($10.5B) > Cloud ($3.8B). Both from same 10-Q filing.

questions.question: Amazon reported that in Q3 2025, North America sales increased 11%, International sales increased 14%, and AWS sales increased 20%, all compared to Q3 2024. Which of Amazon's three segments grew the fastest, and what does this suggest about the company's growth drivers?
questions.expected_answer: AWS grew the fastest at 20%, followed by International at 14% and North America at 11%. This suggests cloud computing remains Amazon's highest-growth segment, outpacing both its domestic and international e-commerce operations by a significant margin.

source.section_hint: Segment sales growth discussion, Q3 2025
source.original_text: AWS sales increased 20% in Q3 2025, and 18% for the nine months ended September 30, 2025 compared to the comparable prior year periods.

questions.notes: Ranking: AWS 20% > International 14% > North America 11%. All three growth rates from AMZN_10-Q_2025-10-31.

questions.question: In Q3 2025, Meta's Family of Apps (FoA) segment generated income from operations of $24,967 million with a 49% operating margin, while the Reality Labs (RL) segment reported a loss from operations of $4,432 million. FoA income from operations grew 15% year-over-year while RL's loss was essentially flat (0% change). Given that Meta's consolidated operating income was $20,535 million (40% margin), what does this tell us about the impact of Reality Labs on Meta's overall profitability?
questions.expected_answer: Reality Labs significantly drags down Meta's overall profitability. FoA alone has a 49% operating margin, but RL's $4,432 million loss reduces the consolidated margin to 40%, a gap of 9 percentage points. RL consumed roughly 18% of FoA's operating income without generating a profit.

source.section_hint: Segment operating results, FoA vs RL, Q3 2025
source.original_text: FoA income from operations in the three and nine months ended September 30, 2025 increased $3.19 billion, or 15%.

questions.notes: Calculation: FoA margin 49% vs consolidated 40% = 9pp drag from RL. RL loss as % of FoA income: $4,432M / $24,967M = ~18%. All from META_10-Q_2025-10-30 segment table.

questions.question: In Q3 2025, Tesla's automotive sales revenue increased 8% year-over-year while energy generation and storage revenue increased 44% year-over-year ($1.04 billion increase). Services and other revenue also increased 25% year-over-year ($685 million increase). Which non-automotive segment is growing faster, and how does this compare to the automotive business?
questions.expected_answer: Energy generation and storage is growing fastest at 44%, well ahead of Services at 25% and Automotive at 8%. Both non-automotive segments are significantly outpacing the core automotive business, suggesting Tesla's revenue is diversifying away from vehicle sales.

source.section_hint: Segment revenue discussion, Q3 2025 year-over-year comparisons
source.original_text: Energy generation and storage revenue increased $1.04 billion, or 44%, in the three months ended September 30, 2025 as compared to the three months ended September 30, 2024.

questions.notes: Growth ranking: Energy 44% > Services 25% > Automotive 8%. All figures from TSLA_10-Q_2025-10-23.

questions.question: Amazon's Q4 2025 guidance stated that operating income is expected to be between $21.0 billion and $26.0 billion, compared with $21.2 billion in Q4 2024. At the low end of guidance, would Amazon's operating income improve, decline, or stay roughly flat compared to Q4 2024? What about at the high end?
questions.expected_answer: At the low end ($21.0B), operating income would decline slightly by $0.2 billion versus Q4 2024's $21.2B. At the high end ($26.0B), it would increase by $4.8 billion. The guidance range thus spans from a small decline to significant growth, indicating uncertainty in the outlook.

source.section_hint: Fourth Quarter 2025 Guidance, operating income
source.original_text: Operating income is expected to be between $21.0 billion and $26.0 billion, compared with $21.2 billion in fourth quarter 2024.

questions.notes: Low end: $21.0B - $21.2B = -$0.2B (decline). High end: $26.0B - $21.2B = +$4.8B (growth). Both figures from AMZN_10-Q_2025-10-31.

questions.question: Amazon's Q4 2025 guidance stated net sales are expected to be between $206.0 billion and $213.0 billion, representing growth of 10% to 13% compared with Q4 2024. The guidance also anticipates a favorable FX impact of approximately 190 basis points. If the FX tailwind were removed, what would the approximate organic growth range be?
questions.expected_answer: The approximate organic growth range would be 8.1% to 11.1%. Subtracting the 190 basis point (1.9 percentage point) FX tailwind from both ends: 10% - 1.9% = 8.1% at the low end, and 13% - 1.9% = 11.1% at the high end.

source.section_hint: Fourth Quarter 2025 Guidance, net sales and FX impact
source.original_text: Net sales are expected to be between $206.0 billion and $213.0 billion, or to grow between 10% and 13% compared with fourth quarter 2024. This guidance anticipates a favorable impact of approximately 190 basis points from foreign exchange rates.

questions.notes: Calculation: 10% - 1.9pp = 8.1% (low). 13% - 1.9pp = 11.1% (high). Both figures from AMZN_10-Q_2025-10-31 guidance section.

questions.question: JPMorgan Chase reported Q3 2025 net interest income (NII) of $24.0 billion (up 2%) and noninterest revenue (NIR) of $22.5 billion (up 17%). The NIR figure included a $7.9 billion net gain related to Visa shares. If the Visa gain were excluded, would NIR still have shown growth over the prior year period, given that total NIR was up 17%?
questions.expected_answer: It depends on the prior-year NIR base. NIR of $22.5 billion represents a 17% increase, implying prior-year NIR was approximately $19.2 billion. Excluding the $7.9 billion Visa gain, adjusted NIR would be approximately $14.6 billion, which would be a decline of roughly 24% from the prior year. The Visa gain more than accounted for the reported NIR growth.

source.section_hint: Net revenue composition, NII and NIR, Q3 2025; Visa gain impact
source.original_text: Total net revenue included a $7.9 billion net gain related to Visa shares.

questions.notes: Calculation: Prior NIR = $22.5B / 1.17 ≈ $19.2B. Adjusted NIR = $22.5B - $7.9B = $14.6B. $14.6B vs $19.2B = decline of ~24%. Visa gain was the primary driver.

questions.question: Alphabet reported operating cash flow of $48.4 billion and capital expenditures of $24.0 billion for Q3 2025 (three months ended September 30, 2025). What is the ratio of capex to operating cash flow, and what does this ratio suggest about Alphabet's investment intensity?
questions.expected_answer: The capex-to-operating-cash-flow ratio is approximately 50% ($24.0B / $48.4B). This means Alphabet is reinvesting roughly half of its operating cash flow into capital expenditures, primarily technical infrastructure, indicating very high investment intensity typical of companies building out AI infrastructure at scale.

source.section_hint: Operating cash flow and capital expenditures, Q3 2025
source.original_text: Operating cash flow was $48.4 billion for the three months ended September 30, 2025. Capital expenditures were $24.0 billion.

questions.notes: Calculation: $24.0B / $48.4B = 49.6% ≈ 50%. Both figures from GOOGL_10-Q_2025-10-30.

questions.question: Tesla reported operating cash flow of $10.93 billion and capital expenditures of $6.13 billion for the nine months ended September 30, 2025. In the prior year period, operating cash flow was $10.11 billion and capex was $8.56 billion. How did Tesla's implied free cash flow (operating cash flow minus capex) change, and what drove the change?
questions.expected_answer: Tesla's implied free cash flow improved significantly, from $1.55 billion ($10.11B - $8.56B) to $4.80 billion ($10.93B - $6.13B), an increase of $3.25 billion. The improvement was primarily driven by a $2.43 billion decrease in capital expenditures, with a smaller $0.82 billion increase in operating cash flow contributing as well.

source.section_hint: Cash flows from operating and investing activities, nine months ended September 30, 2025 vs 2024
source.original_text: Our cash flows provided by operating activities were $10.93 billion during the nine months ended September 30, 2025, compared to $10.11 billion during the same period ended September 30, 2024. Capital expenditures amounted to $6.13 billion during the nine months ended September 30, 2025, compared to $8.56 billion during the same period ended September 30, 2024.

questions.question: Meta reported that headcount was 75,945 as of June 30, 2025 (up 7% year-over-year) and 78,450 as of September 30, 2025 (up 8% year-over-year). Revenue grew 22% in Q2 2025 and 26% in Q3 2025. Is Meta's revenue growing faster or slower than its headcount? What does this suggest about operational efficiency?
questions.expected_answer: Revenue is growing much faster than headcount. In Q3 2025, revenue grew 26% while headcount grew 8%, a gap of 18 percentage points. This indicates increasing operational efficiency — Meta is generating significantly more revenue per employee over time, suggesting its investments in AI and automation are improving productivity.

source.section_hint: Headcount and revenue growth, Q2 and Q3 2025
source.original_text: Headcount was 78,450 as of September 30, 2025, an increase of 8% year-over-year.

questions.notes: Q3: Revenue growth 26% vs headcount growth 8% = 18pp gap. Q2: 22% vs 7% = 15pp gap. Revenue consistently outpacing headcount.

questions.question: NVIDIA disclosed that in Q3 FY2026, four direct customers each accounted for more than 10% of total revenue: Customer A at 22%, Customer B at 15%, Customer C at 13%, and Customer D at 11%. What is the combined revenue concentration from these four customers, and what financial risk does this level of concentration pose?
questions.expected_answer: The four customers collectively represent 61% of NVIDIA's total revenue (22% + 15% + 13% + 11%). This extreme concentration poses significant risk — the loss or significant reduction in orders from any single customer could materially impact NVIDIA's financial results. Customer A alone at 22% represents over one-fifth of all revenue.

source.section_hint: Customer concentration disclosure, Q3 FY2026
source.original_text: For the third quarter of fiscal year 2026, four direct customers with sales greater than 10% of total revenue included: Customer A at 22%, Customer B at 15%, Customer C at 13%, and Customer D at 11%.

questions.notes: Calculation: 22% + 15% + 13% + 11% = 61% total concentration. Over half of revenue from just 4 customers.

questions.question: JPMorgan Chase reported Q3 2025 provision for credit losses of $3.4 billion and net charge-offs of $2.6 billion. The provision exceeded actual charge-offs by $810 million, which was added to the allowance for credit losses. What does it mean when a bank's provision for credit losses exceeds its actual charge-offs?
questions.expected_answer: When provision exceeds charge-offs, the bank is building reserves because it expects future credit losses to increase. The $810 million excess ($3.4B provision minus $2.6B charge-offs) was added to the allowance, signaling that JPM's management anticipates worsening credit conditions and is proactively reserving capital to absorb expected future losses.

source.section_hint: Provision for credit losses and net charge-offs, Q3 2025
source.original_text: The provision for credit losses was $3.4 billion. Net charge-offs of $2.6 billion were up $506 million. The net addition to the allowance for credit losses was $810 million.

questions.notes: Calculation: $3.4B provision - $2.6B charge-offs = $810M reserve build. Building reserves = expectation of future losses increasing.

description: Financial Fine-Tuning Laboratory eval set v1.0. Ground truth sourced from real SEC 10-Q filings and expert-labeled FinancialPhraseBank dataset.
